articles.title: Fox Agrees to Buy Roku in $22 Billion Streaming Deal
articles.why: Fox acquiring Roku for $22 billion is a genuine power move for control of the living room — giving Murdoch's empire direct access to 100 million households, a massive advertising business, and the viewing data that comes with it. This reshapes the streaming landscape in a concrete, immediate way.
articles.hook: Fox just bought Roku for $22 billion, giving Rupert Murdoch's company a direct pipeline into 100 million households and their viewing data.

articles.title: How Fox’s $22 Billion Deal For Roku Raises The Floor For Its Content, Advertising Revenue & Creator Division
articles.why: The Fox-Roku deal is one of the most consequential streaming power moves in years — Deadline's analysis goes beyond the headline to explain how it reshapes content distribution, advertising, and the creator economy in one stroke. This is the deal that changes what you see when you turn on your TV.
articles.hook: Fox just bought Roku for $22 billion, which means Rupert Murdoch's company now controls the home screen that 80 million American households see the moment they switch on their television.

articles.title: Fox to acquire Roku in $22B deal
articles.why: Fox acquiring Roku for $22 billion is a direct power grab at the living room — giving Rupert Murdoch's media empire ownership of the dominant streaming hardware platform and access to millions of cord-cutters. This reshapes the streaming wars in a structural way that goes beyond any single show or deal.
articles.hook: Fox just bought Roku for $22 billion, meaning the company that owns Fox News now controls the platform sitting in front of your TV.

articles.title: UK will ban social media for children under 16
articles.why: The UK's sweeping social media ban for under-16s — covering TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, Snapchat, X, and Facebook — is a landmark policy moment that signals English-speaking democracies are now prepared to use hard legal force to regulate children's access to platforms. With 5 independent live signals confirmed, this is a genuinely live cultural reckoning.
articles.hook: The UK just banned everyone under 16 from TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, and Facebook — the most sweeping social media restriction in a major Western democracy.

articles.title: Fox wants to take over your TV — and the tech inside it
articles.why: Fox acquiring Roku isn't just a hardware deal — it's a direct power move on the living room operating system, giving Rupert Murdoch's company control over the interface through which over 100 million households navigate all of streaming. The Verge's framing as a 'takeover' is exactly right.
articles.hook: Fox just bought the company that runs the home screen on over 100 million TVs — meaning it now controls what you see before you even open Netflix.

articles.title: The UK Places a Sweeping Ban on Social Media for Kids Under 16
articles.why: The UK's social media ban for under-16s is the most sweeping digital age restriction enacted by a major democracy, covering TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, Snapchat, X, and Facebook — and it sets a template that other countries are watching closely.
articles.hook: The UK just banned social media for anyone under 16, covering every major platform, and it's the most aggressive move any major democracy has made to reshape how young people exist online.

articles.title: These are the countries moving to ban social media for children
articles.why: As the UK joins Australia in banning social media for under-16s — covering TikTok, Instagram, YouTube, and more — a clear international consensus is forming around child safety online. This is the policy story that will define how the next generation grows up with the internet.
articles.hook: The UK just banned social media for anyone under 16, and is considering overnight curfews too — the most aggressive move any Western democracy has made on kids and tech.

articles.title: UK to ban social media for kids under 16, may impose overnight curfews
articles.why: The UK's social media ban for under-16s is the most sweeping child-safety policy any major democracy has enacted — going further than Australia's lead and adding the possibility of overnight curfews. This is the article that explains what the ban actually covers and how it would work.
articles.hook: The UK's under-16 social media ban doesn't just apply to TikTok and Instagram — it covers YouTube too, and the government is weighing overnight curfews on top of that.

articles.title: Facebook's new AI tools offer more of the same, with photo-editing and question-answering capabilities
articles.why: Meta rolling out AI Mode on Facebook — pulling from public posts across its platforms to generate answers — is a concrete escalation in how social data gets monetized and repurposed, with real implications for privacy and the future of search.
articles.hook: Meta's new Facebook AI Mode uses your public posts to generate answers for other people's questions — no opt-out mentioned.

articles.title: How the Strait of Hormuz reopening could unfold if the U.S.-Iran deal is implemented
articles.why: A clear-eyed look at what actually happens to global oil markets if the Hormuz strait reopens — with tanker traffic data and expert analysis — is genuinely useful context for one of the most consequential geopolitical outcomes currently in motion.
articles.hook: Even if the Iran deal holds, analysts say Hormuz traffic won't quickly return to prewar levels — the shipping industry doesn't trust ceasefires it can't verify.

articles.title: Oil tumbles on US-Iran deal framework: How one trader is playing the move
articles.why: Oil prices tumbling on the Iran deal framework — with airline stocks rallying toward yearly highs — is the clearest market signal yet that traders believe the deal is real, with concrete downstream effects already visible in commodity and equity markets.
articles.hook: Oil just dropped sharply on the Iran deal news and airline stocks are hitting yearly highs — traders are pricing in a world where Hormuz reopens.
